title: chore(tools): Rewrite gh-issues from C++ to Python
body:
- [x] Create Python script `tools/gh-issues.py` with all commands from the C++ tool
- [x] Remove C++ gh-issues tool and PowerShell script
- [x] Remove gh-issues from root `CMakeLists.txt`
- [x] Update all documentation
- [x] Code review feedback addressed (Callable type hint, consistent punctuation)
- [x] Fix shell injection: change `run_gh` from `shell=True` string to `shell=False` arg list
- [x] Fix `--no-milestone` flag: replace with `--search "no:milestone"`
- [x] Fix `show_chain` error handling: distinguish failed root fetch from independent issue
- [x] Fix `parse_int_list`: reject non-positive issue numbers
- [x] Fix exit code propagation: handlers return bool, main() maps failures to non-zero exit

title: Berry solidification made possible with Python runtime
body:
## Description:

The Berry runtime was ported from `C` to `Python` which allows to move solidification stage into the toolchain. The goal is eventually to introduce new features like conditional compilation (smaller firmwares) and text localization.

The porting took less than a week using Kiro and Claude Opus 4.6. It generates a byte-to-byte compatible solidification source code. 

The solidification is not integrated in the toolchain yet.

As a side effect, `int64` support was ported to plain `C` code not using `lv_mapping` which makes it also faster.

## Checklist:
  - [x] The pull request is done against the latest development branch
  - [x] Only relevant files were touched
  - [x] Only one feature/fix was added per PR and the code change compiles without warnings
  - [ ] The code change is tested and works with Tasmota core ESP8266 V.2.7.8
  - [x] The code change is tested and works with Tasmota core ESP32 V.3.1.10 / V.3.3.7
  - [x] I accept the [CLA](https://github.com/arendst/Tasmota/blob/development/CONTRIBUTING.md#contributor-license-agreement-cla).

_NOTE: The code change must pass CI tests. **Your PR cannot be merged unless tests pass**_

title: consolidate file downloading from C++ to Python
Move sensor metadata from C++ to Python schema (fixes compilation with ESPHome 2026.3)
body:
ESPHome 2026.3 removed undocumented `EntityBase` runtime setters such as `set_unit_of_measurement()` and `set_device_class()`. This component still used those setters in several custom sensor constructors, which caused the build failure.

This PR migrates those sensor metadata fields to the Python schema/codegen layer and removes the obsolete C++ setter calls.

Affected sensors:
- `CompressorFrequencySensor` (Hz, frequency)
- `InputPowerSensor` (W, power)
- `kWhSensor` (kWh, energy)
- `RuntimeHoursSensor` (h, duration)
- `OutsideAirTemperatureSensor` (°C, temperature)

## Notes

This follows the migration path expected by ESPHome 2026.3: entity metadata is now provided through schema/codegen and packed into `configure_entity_()` instead of being set from runtime C++ constructors.

⚠️ **This is not a cosmetic change.** The sensor metadata path changes entirely: from C++ runtime to Python codegen. Please verify carefully:

- [x] All five sensors appear correctly in Home Assistant with proper unit and device_class (not "unknown" or blank)
- [x] Historical data / long-term statistics are not disrupted (same entity IDs, same units; HA should see continuity)
- [x] YAML overrides still work (e.g. setting a custom `unit_of_measurement` on any of these sensors)
- [x] No regressions on compressor_frequency reporting during active heating/cooling cycles
- [x] kWh sensor still uses `state_class: total_increasing` (critical for energy dashboard)

It would also be good to test it with older ESPHome versions to confirm backward compatibility. I would expect it to remain compatible, but it’s still worth verifying explicitly.

title: Move TH1IntegrateWithPartialBins from C++ to Python
body:
ROOT.HDTV.TH1IntegrateWithPartialBins() is only used in histogram.py.
Since it is a small and easy function, it can be implemented directly in PyROOT and does not need to be compiled.

---

This patch isn't covered by the test suite. ~I'm also unsure how to trigger the function manually.~ Manual tested (https://github.com/janmayer/hdtv/pull/52#issuecomment-3796839728)

title: Move Gaussian splatting autograd and pipeline logic from C++ to Python
body:
## Summary

- Port all C++ autograd functions (projection, rasterization, SH evaluation) to pure Python `torch.autograd.Function` implementations in a new `_gaussian_autograd.py` module, making the differentiable pipeline easier to extend and debug.
- Eliminate the C++ `GaussianSplat3d` class entirely: the Python `GaussianSplat3d` class now directly manages `torch.Tensor` attributes instead of delegating to a C++ `_impl` object.
- Rewrite `deduplicatePixels` as a pure Python function using PyTorch ops, removing its C++ binding and implementation.
- Refactor PLY I/O to return a flat tuple of tensors (no intermediate struct), and update the viewer to accept individual tensors directly instead of a C++ object.
- Remove ~7,600 lines of dead C++ code (`GaussianSplat3d.{h,cpp}`, `detail/autograd/*`, `DeduplicatePixelsTest.cpp`, `GaussianSplat3dCameraApiTest.cpp`).
- Simplify pybind bindings to only expose low-level dispatch functions; all autograd wiring now lives in Python.

## Test plan

- [x] All 135 Python unit tests pass (`cd tests && pytest unit/test_gaussian_splat_3d.py -v`)
- [x] All 21 C++ gtests pass (`ctest --test-dir build/.../src/tests`)
- [x] CI passes (codestyle, DCO, full test matrix)

title: Migrate MMHC from R/C++ to Python with Streamlit app and notebooks
body:
Replace the entire R package + Rcpp/C++ backend with a modern Python implementation using NumPy, SciPy, Numba, and NetworkX.

Key improvements over the original:
- No conditioning variable limit (was capped at 3)
- Cycle detection during hill-climbing (was missing)
- Reproducible via seeded RNG (was srand(time(NULL)))
- Configurable parameters via MMHCConfig dataclass
- 60 pytest tests at 92% coverage
- Streamlit app with Excel/CSV upload and interactive pyvis graphs
- Two Jupyter notebooks (quickstart + custom data)

title: Port EMU7800 Atari 7800/2600 emulator from C# to Python
body:
Complete port of the EMU7800 C# emulator to Python, including:

- MOS 6502 CPU emulation (all 256 opcodes, addressing modes, BCD, interrupts)
- TIA graphics chip (2600 video rendering, collision detection, WSYNC)
- MARIA graphics chip (7800 video with DMA, 6 render modes, DLL/DL)
- PIA/RIOT 6532 (timer, I/O ports, 128B RAM, controller/console switch reading)
- AddressSpace with page-granularity memory mapping and snooper device
- InputState with double-buffered controller/console-switch state
- TIASound (2-channel) and PokeySound (4-channel) audio emulation
- 27+ cartridge mappers for both 2600 and 7800
- Machine hierarchy: MachineBase -> Machine2600/Machine7800 -> NTSC/PAL variants
- Platform layer: pygame window, audio output, keyboard/gamepad input handling
- Shell services: ROM loading with .a78 header parsing, machine factory
- CLI entry point with argparse for loading and running ROMs

https://claude.ai/code/session_01174A385inAc7io8bZKf2Ek

title: [bugfix] Rewrite transfer metrics from C++ to Python side
body:
Previously, transfer metrics (ops_total, bytes_total) were recorded in the C++ layer (transfer.cu, transfer_ssd.cpp, gds_manager.cpp) via MetricsManager macros. Although C++ has its own Prometheus exporter (port 8081), the actual data transfer code runs in forked worker subprocesses, which do not initialize the C++ Prometheus metrics server. As a result, these transfer metrics were never collected at all.

Key changes:
- Rewrite transfer metrics collection entirely on the Python side with a post-completion approach, recording metrics in the main process after transfer operations complete
- Add `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to Python collector for tracking data transfer throughput
- Remove `flexkv_cpp_transfer_ops_total` and `flexkv_cpp_transfer_bytes_total` counters from C++ MetricsManager

Changes include:
- Extended `CompletedOp` in `flexkv/common/transfer.py` with `transfer_type`, `num_blocks`, and `num_bytes` fields
- Compute transfer bytes in `TransferEngine` (`transfer_engine.py`) when an op finishes
- Record all three counters (ops_total, blocks_total, bytes_total) via `record_transfer_completed()` in `KVTaskManager._update_tasks()` (`kvtask.py`), ensuring metrics are collected in the main process
- Added `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to `flexkv/metrics/collector.py`
- Removed old `_record_transfer_ops()` from `cache_engine.py`
- Removed C++ transfer metric recording from `transfer.cu`, `transfer_ssd.cpp`, and `gds_manager.cpp`
- Updated Grafana dashboard (`flexkv-demo.json`): added Transfer Throughput panel, replaced C++ Transfer Throughput with Cache Blocks Rate panel
- Updated monitoring documentation at `docs/monitoring/README_en.md` (English) and `docs/monitoring/README_zh.md` (Chinese)
